questions.question: A measuring cylinder contains $20\text{ cm}^3$ of water. When an irregular stone of mass $150\text{ g}$ is submerged, the level rises to $50\text{ cm}^3$. Calculate the density of the stone.
questions.explanation:
Volume of stone $= 50 - 20 = 30\text{ cm}^3$. Density $= 150\text{ g} / 30\text{ cm}^3 = 5.0\text{ g/cm}^3$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the final water level ($50$) as the volume of the stone.

questions.question: To convert a density value from $g/cm^3$ to the SI unit $kg/m^3$, you should:
questions.explanation:
$1\text{ g/cm}^3$ is equivalent to $1000\text{ kg/m}^3$. 

**Common mistake:** Dividing by 1000, which is the standard conversion for grams to kilograms alone.

questions.question: A measuring cylinder has a mass of $60.0\text{ g}$. When $30\text{ cm}^3$ of olive oil is poured into it, the total mass is $87.6\text{ g}$. What is the density of the olive oil?
questions.explanation:
Mass of oil $= 87.6 - 60.0 = 27.6\text{ g}$. Density $= 27.6 / 30 = 0.92\text{ g/cm}^3$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the total mass ($87.6$) instead of the mass of the liquid only.

questions.question: Liquid A (density $800\text{ kg/m}^3$), Liquid B (density $1000\text{ kg/m}^3$), and Liquid C (density $1200\text{ kg/m}^3$) are immiscible. If poured into a cylinder, which order will they settle in from top to bottom?
questions.explanation:
Immiscible liquids settle with the least dense on top and the most dense at the bottom. 

**Common mistake:** Reversing the order and placing the densest liquid on top.

questions.question: An object floats in Seawater (density $1100\text{ kg/m}^3$) but sinks in Paraffin (density $700\text{ kg/m}^3$). What can be deduced about the object's density?
options: It is between $700\text{ kg/m}^3$ and $1100\text{ kg/m}^3$
questions.explanation:
Since it floats in seawater, its density $< 1100$. Since it sinks in paraffin, its density $> 700$. 

**Common mistake:** Assuming floating means it must be less dense than both liquids.
